answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem "Swami Ram Tirath" from *Bang-E-Dara Part 2* (ch. 23), the speaker addresses the titular Hindu mystic (Swami Ram Tirath, 1873–1906, a revered figure known for his Vedantic teachings and ascetic life) as one who has achieved profound spiritual annihilation (*fana*) and union with the divine, transcending worldly distinctions through ecstatic love.[^1]

The poem opens by likening him to a "restless drop" that merges into the river (a classic Sufi metaphor for the soul's dissolution into the ocean of divinity), transforming from a mere pearl into one "beyond price."[^2] Iqbal marvels at how gracefully Ram Tirath "unveiled the secret of color and scent"—esoteric knowledge of unity beneath multiplicity—while the poet-speaker confesses to remaining "a prisoner of their distinctions."[^3]

Further stanzas portray his death not as loss but exaltation: his annihilation turns "life's clamor" into the "tumult of Judgment Day," and his extinguished spark becomes "the fire-temple of Azar" (a reference to the prophet Abraham's rejection of idolatry in fire, symbolizing purified faith).[^4] Iqbal extols the "negation of being" (*la ilaha*, "there is no god") as a "wonder of the knowing heart," revealing the affirming pearl of *illa-Allah* ("but Allah") hidden within; to the blind, this ultimate reality remains veiled, akin to quicksilver stabilizing into silver once "restlessness ceases."[^5]

The climax invokes Love (*ishq*) as the path's fulfillment: the "Abraham of Love shatters the idol of the Self," and reason's cure lies in the intoxicating ecstasy of Love's "Tasnim" (a paradisal spring in Islamic tradition, evoking Sufi rapture).[^6] Overall, Iqbal celebrates Ram Tirath as a universal mystic exemplar, bridging Hindu *Vedanta* and Islamic *Sufism* in self-transcending devotion.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 23: "Swami Ram Tirath" (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-d1bdd5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-d1bdd5).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-cb1e8e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-cb1e8e).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-420405](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-420405).

[^5]: Ibid., [p-ef7cac](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-ef7cac) and [p-fc9342](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-fc9342).

[^6]: Ibid., [p-7de605](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/23-swami-ram-tirath/translation/#p-7de605).
